title: Local topology of singularities and MMP
speaker: Chenyang Xu
tags: Workshop on Fundamental Groups and Periods

proof_stages.label: Resolution dual complex of the link
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the link of a singularity, recalls that a log resolution produces a simple-normal-crossing exceptional divisor, and explains that the homotopy type of its dual complex is independent of the chosen resolution via weak factorization.

proof_stages.label: Target invariants and klt/dlt language
proof_stages.summary: The speaker states the goal of replacing the resolution-dependent dual complex by a more canonical MMP object, states the main theorems, and introduces klt and dlt singularities together with dual complexes for dlt boundaries.

proof_stages.label: Dlt modifications as canonical MMP endpoints
proof_stages.summary: A dlt modification is defined as a dlt partial resolution that is relatively nef over the singularity, its existence is obtained from MMP, and any two such modifications are shown to have PL-homeomorphic dual complexes by counting only discrepancy-one divisors.

proof_stages.label: Weight-sublevel collapse under MMP
proof_stages.summary: To prove that the dlt-modification dual complex has the same homotopy type as the log-resolution dual complex, a weight function on exceptional divisors is introduced and MMP is analyzed to show that every sublevel complex collapses to the minimum stratum complex.

proof_stages.label: Klt contractibility via one Kollár component
proof_stages.summary: For a klt singularity, a general boundary is added so that a dlt modification extracts a single irreducible Kollár component, and the collapse theorem then implies that the original dual complex is collapsible, hence contractible.

proof_stages.label: Profinite local fundamental group via log Fano boundedness
proof_stages.summary: The Kollár component is studied by adjunction as a log Fano variety; pulling it through a tower of quasi-étale covers and using boundedness of log Fano degrees forces the tower to stabilize, giving finiteness of the profinite local fundamental group.

proof_stages.label: Motivic zeta maximum poles via minimal weights
proof_stages.summary: For a log resolution of a hypersurface, the same discrepancy/multiplicity weight function is used to show that any maximal-order candidate pole corresponds to a maximal cell whose common weight must be the minimum weight, namely the log canonical threshold.

examples.name: Triangle dual complex under blow-ups
examples.note: A threefold with three intersecting divisors is drawn as a 2-simplex to illustrate how blowing up a stratum subdivides the dual complex while an admissible non-stratum blow-up adds a homotopically trivial cone.
examples.engagement_reason: He draws the triangular dual complex for three intersecting divisors and follows two blow-ups, identifying subdivision versus adding a cone/tail and their homotopy effect.

passes.reason: A standard 2-simplex dual complex is used to illustrate blow-up subdivisions/cone additions preserving homotopy.
The standard triangular 2-simplex has full vertex-permutation symmetry and is used only to illustrate blow-up subdivisions/cone additions preserving homotopy.

An ordinary 2-simplex dual complex is used to illustrate blow-up subdivision and cone attachment, not to refute a claim.
The starting dual complex is a maximally regular 2-simplex, used illustratively to show blow-up subdivision/coning preserves homotopy.
A filled triangle dual complex for three SNC components is an ordinary illustrative simplex, used to show blow-up subdivisions/cone attachments preserve homotopy.

examples.name: Surface singularities with a minimal resolution
examples.note: The surface case is used as a contrast where a best resolution exists, motivating why higher-dimensional singularities require MMP-based replacements.
examples.engagement_reason: Surface singularities and their minimal resolutions are cited as the case with a canonical best resolution, without working through a particular surface example.

passes.reason: The resolution is chosen minimal/canonical among resolutions of a surface singularity, and is used as a contrast rather than a refutation.

examples.name: Regular versus non-regular cell complex sketch
examples.note: A simple drawing distinguishes regular cell complexes from non-regular ones, making precise the kind of complex asserted in the main invariant theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: He only uses sketched cell complexes to illustrate the definition by saying which are regular or not, without checking the condition in detail.

passes.reason: The non-regular sketch is a negative example where the regular-cell boundary injectivity condition fails.

examples.name: Dlt boundary with smooth generic intersections
examples.note: A divisor with singularities away from generic intersections is sketched to clarify the definition of a dlt pair and why its strata can be used for a dual complex.
examples.engagement_reason: The dlt boundary picture is described generically as having smooth generic intersections and modified log resolutions, but no concrete dlt example is analyzed.

passes.reason: A standard dlt boundary with generically smooth intersections is used to illustrate the definition.

examples.name: Threefold flip and simple-collapse picture
examples.note: The flip of a curve where two surfaces meet shows how MMP can change topology, while the presence of an auxiliary divisor f0 forces the disappearing cells to come in pairs and reveals the simple-collapse mechanism used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through a threefold flip picture, tracks how the dual complex changes, and explains why the added divisor produces a simple collapse rather than a topology change.

passes.reason: The raw threefold flip is shown turning an edge into disconnected vertices, exposing that an unqualified MMP step can change dual-complex topology.
The threefold flip is an extremal-ray MMP operation and is first used as the raw topology-changing edge-to-two-points obstruction before the controlled simple-collapse case.
The flip is an MMP extremal-ray operation and is used as an enemy case showing topology can change unless the extra divisor makes it a simple collapse.
The flip is an extremal-ray MMP operation, and the raw topology-changing flip is used as the warning case before the f0 simple-collapse argument.
A threefold flip is an MMP operation along an extremal ray, and the bare flip is invoked as a topology-changing obstruction before the auxiliary simple-collapse picture.
A threefold flip is along an extremal ray, and the raw edge-to-two-points flip is used to show MMP can change dual-complex topology before the added divisor makes it a simple collapse.
The threefold flip is used to show that raw MMP can change the dual-complex topology before the added divisor turns the step into a simple collapse, and flips are extremal-ray MMP steps.
The flip is an extremal-ray MMP operation cited first as a topology-changing obstruction, before the added divisor turns the relevant case into a simple collapse.
the flip of an MMP extremal curve is used to show that arbitrary MMP steps can change the dual complex before the added divisor makes it a simple collapse
the flip contracts an MMP extremal ray and is shown as the topology-changing enemy case before the simple-collapse fix
the flip picture is used first to show that raw MMP can change the dual-complex topology, before the added divisor turns the change into a simple collapse.
